Jeremy Howard.summary: Jeremy Howard (born November 13, 1973 in London) is an Australian data scientist, entrepreneur, educator, and researcher. He co‑founded fast.ai, a nonprofit deep learning research group dedicated to democratizing artificial intelligence. He developed the ULMFiT algorithm, significantly advancing transfer learning in NLP, and served as President and Chief Scientist at Kaggle after top‑ranking in its machine learning competitions. He also founded Enlitic, FastMail, Optimal Decisions Group, and in December 2023, he launched Answer.AI — an AI R&D lab focused on turning foundational research into practical products.
keyPoints: Born November 13, 1973 in London; educated at the University of Melbourne in philosophy before shifting to data science and entrepreneurship ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jeremy_Howard_%28entrepreneur%29?utm_source=openai)).

Co‑founded fast.ai in October 2016, offering a free MOOC ‘Practical Deep Learning for Coders’ and the fastai software library built on PyTorch ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast.ai?utm_source=openai)).

Developed the ULMFiT algorithm, pioneering modern NLP transfer learning and underpinning large language models such as ChatGPT and Google Gemini ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jeremy_Howard_%28entrepreneur%29?utm_source=openai)).

Served as President and Chief Scientist at Kaggle after winning top global ranking in its competitions, and founded companies including Enlitic (deep learning in medicine), FastMail, and Optimal Decisions Group; in December 2023 launched Answer.AI with a $10M investment ([fast.ai](https://www.fast.ai/about.html?utm_source=openai)).

Jeremy Howard co-founder of fast.ai and.canonical: Jeremy Howard co-founder of fast.ai and deep learning accessibility
Jeremy Howard co-founder of fast.ai and.summary: Jeremy Howard is an Australian data scientist, entrepreneur, educator, and co‑founder of fast.ai—a nonprofit research institute launched in October 2016 with Rachel Thomas dedicated to democratizing deep learning. He developed the ULMFiT approach, taught accessible deep‑learning courses, and created the popular fastai library to make deep learning approachable for a broad audience.
keyPoints: Jeremy Howard co‑founded fast.ai in October 2016 with Rachel Thomas to "democratize deep learning" through free courses, research, tools, and community ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast.ai?utm_source=openai)).
He developed ULMFiT, a transfer‑learning method foundational to modern language models like ChatGPT and Google Gemini ([fast.ai](https://www.fast.ai/about.html?utm_source=openai)).

Fast.ai’s Practical Deep Learning for Coders is a widely used free MOOC that enables learners with only basic Python to build deep‑learning models ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fast.ai?utm_source=openai)).

He also created the fastai library, an open‑source, high‑level deep‑learning framework built atop PyTorch, designed for ease of use and accessibility ([fast.ai](https://www.fast.ai/about.html?utm_source=openai)).

fast.ai.summary: fast.ai is an organization that offers practical deep learning courses designed to make advanced AI concepts accessible to learners with minimal technical background. Their most well-known course, "Practical Deep Learning for Coders," emphasizes hands-on coding and rapid prototyping, using the fastai library built on top of PyTorch. The content is frequently updated to reflect advances in AI, and the courses are offered free of charge. The fast.ai approach focuses on practical skills, inclusivity, and breaking down barriers to AI education.
keyPoints: fast.ai provides free, practical deep learning courses accessible to beginners.
The flagship course, 'Practical Deep Learning for Coders,' teaches using the fastai library and PyTorch.
Course material emphasizes hands-on projects and rapid prototyping, rather than just theory.
fast.ai aims to make AI education accessible to a diverse audience by lowering technical barriers.

Education at Melbourne Grammar and Unive.canonical: Education at Melbourne Grammar and University of Melbourne
Education at Melbourne Grammar and Unive.summary: Melbourne Grammar School (MGS) is a leading independent Anglican boys’ school with a strong tradition in humanities, particularly Philosophy and Religious Studies, taught by distinguished educators. Many of its alumni (“Old Melburnians”) go on to study at the University of Melbourne (UniMelb), which has produced numerous notable figures across fields from politics to science and the arts.
keyPoints: Melbourne Grammar emphasizes humanities, with subjects like philosophy taught via a structured curriculum that explores ethical frameworks, critical thinking, and historiographical perspectives ([mgs.vic.edu.au](https://www.mgs.vic.edu.au/learning/our-approach/teaching-and-learning/humanities-and-social-sciences?utm_source=openai)).

Staff such as Justin Glenn (MGS alumnus and Head of Philosophy and Religious Studies since 2018) and Felicity McCutcheon (former Head) reflect the school’s notable expertise in philosophy education ([mgs.vic.edu.au](https://www.mgs.vic.edu.au/about/our-people/staff-stories/justin-glenn?utm_source=openai)).

Many Melbourne Grammar students enroll at the University of Melbourne; the university itself has a distinguished alumni network including former Prime Ministers (e.g. Julia Gillard), Nobel laureates (e.g. Peter Doherty, Elizabeth Blackburn), and cultural icons like Cate Blanchett and Ronny Chieng ([unimelb.edu.au](https://www.unimelb.edu.au/alumni/stay-connected/global-community/notable-alumni?utm_source=openai)).

One example of a pathway intertwining both institutions is Will Flintoft (MGS OM 2021), who studied Mathematics and Philosophy at Harvard, and was awarded a Rhodes Scholarship for Oxford in 2026—highlighting the academic and philosophical strengths fostered at Melbourne Grammar that align with university-level excellence ([linkedin.com](https://www.linkedin.com/posts/melbourne-grammar-school_melbournegrammarschool-melbournegrammar-activity-7374636613643137025-MYI2?utm_source=openai)).

Birth in London and move to Melbourne in.canonical: Birth in London and move to Melbourne in 1976
Birth in London and move to Melbourne in.summary: The biographical concept “born in London and moved to Melbourne in 1976” is confirmed for Briony Behets, an English-born Australian actress. She was born in London in 1951 and settled in Melbourne after relocating to Australia, where she became active in theatre and television throughout the 1970s.
keyPoints: Briony Behets was born in London, United Kingdom on 21 July 1951 and studied at the Guildhall School of Music and Drama in London before traveling overseas ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Briony_Behets?utm_source=openai)).

After moving to Australia, she lived in Melbourne and became known as an actress in television soap operas during the 1970s and 1980s; notably, she received an AFI Award nomination for her role in The Trespassers (1976) ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Briony_Behets?utm_source=openai)).

ULMFiT helped pave the way for modern la.canonical: ULMFiT helped pave the way for modern language models like GPT
ULMFiT helped pave the way for modern la.summary: ULMFiT (Universal Language Model Fine‑Tuning), introduced in early 2018 by Jeremy Howard and Sebastian Ruder, pioneered the use of transfer learning in NLP through a pre‑training then fine‑tuning paradigm. Built on an AWD‑LSTM architecture, it introduced techniques like discriminative fine‑tuning, slanted triangular learning rates, and gradual unfreezing to enable efficient task adaptation with limited labeled data. This paradigm laid foundational groundwork for later transformer‑based models like GPT and BERT to adopt and scale pre‑training and fine‑tuning approaches, establishing a standard training methodology in modern language modeling.
keyPoints: Introduced in January 2018, ULMFiT was among the first effective demonstrations of transfer learning in NLP, significantly reducing error rates (18–24%) across text classification tasks and matching performance of models trained on 100× more data with only 100 labeled examples ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/1801.06146?utm_source=openai)).

ULMFiT’s architecture used AWD‑LSTM and novel fine‑tuning techniques (discriminative fine‑tuning, slanted triangular learning rates, gradual unfreezing) to mitigate catastrophic forgetting and enable efficient adaptation to downstream tasks ([medium.com](https://medium.com/%40mukul.mschauhan/a-tale-of-three-models-how-transformers-bert-and-ulmfit-took-nlp-by-storm-58080f1459b6?utm_source=openai)).

Its success popularized the pre‑train then fine‑tune paradigm in NLP, inspiring and paving the way for transformer‑based models like GPT and BERT to adopt similar approaches at much larger scales ([home.mlops.community](https://home.mlops.community/public/blogs/pretraining-breaking-down-the-modern-llm-training-pipeline?utm_source=openai)).

Some analysis even considers ULMFiT as the first true large‑language‑model (LLM), predating GPT‑1 by several months and qualifying as an early example of a next‑word‑predictor adaptable across tasks ([getcoai.com](https://getcoai.com/news/ulmfit-not-gpt-1-was-the-first-true-llm-according-to-new-analysis/?utm_source=openai)).

ULMFiT pioneered transfer learning and f.canonical: ULMFiT pioneered transfer learning and fine-tuning in NLP
ULMFiT pioneered transfer learning and f.summary: ULMFiT (Universal Language Model Fine-tuning), introduced by Jeremy Howard and Sebastian Ruder in their 2018 ACL paper “Universal Language Model Fine-tuning for Text Classification,” pioneered the use of transfer learning with fine-tuning for NLP tasks. It adapted techniques from computer vision—pretraining a language model on a large general corpus, then fine-tuning it for downstream tasks—to NLP, significantly improving performance even on small datasets. The approach introduced key innovations like discriminative fine-tuning, slanted triangular learning rates, and gradual unfreezing to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.
keyPoints: ULMFiT was one of the first successful transfer learning frameworks in NLP, showing that pretraining a language model on a general corpus then fine‑tuning it on downstream tasks yields strong results ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/1801.06146?utm_source=openai)).

It demonstrated significant performance gains (18–24% error reduction on multiple text classification tasks) and strong sample efficiency, achieving performance comparable to training from scratch with 100× more labeled data ([arxiv.org](https://arxiv.org/abs/1801.06146?utm_source=openai)).

ULMFiT introduced practical fine‑tuning strategies—discriminative fine‑tuning, slanted triangular learning rates, and gradual unfreezing—to prevent catastrophic forgetting during adaptation ([medium.com](https://medium.com/%40mukul.mschauhan/a-tale-of-three-models-how-transformers-bert-and-ulmfit-took-nlp-by-storm-58080f1459b6?utm_source=openai)).

It paved the way for widespread adoption of transfer‑learning paradigms in NLP, influencing subsequent models like ELMo, GPT, and especially Transformer‑based models such as BERT ([mbrenndoerfer.com](https://mbrenndoerfer.com/writing/elmo-ulmfit-transfer-learning-natural-language-processing?utm_source=openai)).

ULMFiT (Universal Language Model Fine-tu.summary: ULMFiT (Universal Language Model Fine-tuning) is a transfer learning approach for natural language processing (NLP) introduced in 2018. It is designed to effectively adapt a pre-trained language model, trained on a large corpus such as Wikipedia, to a variety of downstream text classification tasks using fine-tuning. ULMFiT dramatically improved performance on many text classification benchmarks and showcased the benefits of transfer learning in NLP. The approach comprises three phases: pretraining an AWD-LSTM language model on a general dataset, fine-tuning the model on the target task’s text, and then fine-tuning for the specific downstream task, with techniques such as discriminative fine-tuning and gradual unfreezing to enable robust adaptation.
keyPoints: ULMFiT introduced a three-stage transfer learning method for NLP: pretraining, target task fine-tuning, and classifier fine-tuning.
It uses techniques like discriminative fine-tuning and gradual unfreezing to adapt pre-trained models effectively with fewer labeled examples.
ULMFiT signified a breakthrough in NLP by achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple text classification benchmarks with minimal task-specific data.

simplifies implementation of complex neu.canonical: simplifies implementation of complex neural networks
simplifies implementation of complex neu.summary: Fastai is a deep learning library built atop PyTorch that significantly simplifies the implementation of complex neural network models. It achieves this through a layered API providing high‐, mid‐, and low‐level abstractions, enabling rapid prototyping with minimal code while retaining flexibility for custom, advanced architectures.
keyPoints: Layered API: Fastai offers high‑level abstractions (e.g., DataBlock, Learner, callbacks) that reduce boilerplate and simplify training pipelines, yet allows low‑level customization when needed ([geeksforgeeks.org](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/unveiling-the-power-of-fastai-a-deep-dive-into-the-versatile-deep-learning-library/?utm_source=openai)).

Domain‑specific APIs: It supplies ready‑to‑use APIs for computer vision, NLP, tabular data, and recommendation systems, streamlining development across varied applications ([geeksforgeeks.org](https://www.geeksforgeeks.org/unveiling-the-power-of-fastai-a-deep-dive-into-the-versatile-deep-learning-library/?utm_source=openai)).

Architectural primitives: Fastai provides building blocks like SequentialEx, MergeLayer, XResNet, and U‑Net abstractions, enabling easy assembly of modern network architectures and rapid experimentation ([mdpi.com](https://www.mdpi.com/2078-2489/11/2/108?utm_source=openai)).

Transfer learning & optimization tools: Fastai integrates features like learning‑rate finder, transfer learning support, callbacks, schedulers, and pre‑trained models to accelerate training workflows and optimize performance ([medium.com](https://medium.com/%40serverwalainfra/introduction-to-fastai-why-use-it-for-deep-learning-7be66a9468a1?utm_source=openai)).

sources.title: FastAI: A Deep Dive
A Layered API for Deep Learning

layered API for deep learning.summary: The concept “layered API for deep learning” as implemented in fastai refers to a carefully structured hierarchy of abstractions—from high-level, easy-to-use interfaces down to low-level customizable components. This design enables users ranging from beginners to researchers to build, extend, and experiment with deep learning models efficiently and flexibly.
keyPoints: fastai’s layered API comprises three levels: a high-level application layer (e.g., `Learner`, `DataLoaders`) offering concise, sensible defaults for tasks like vision or text; a mid-level API providing core components such as callbacks, optimizers, and data-processing primitives; and a low-level foundation built on composable abstractions like type-dispatch, semantic tensors, and transform pipelines ([fast.ai](https://www.fast.ai/posts/2020-02-13-fastai-A-Layered-API-for-Deep-Learning.html?utm_source=openai)).

This architecture balances ease of use with hackability: beginners can train state-of-the-art models in just a few lines of code, while advanced users can peel back layers to customize or replace internals without rewriting everything ([mdpi.com](https://www.mdpi.com/2078-2489/11/2/108?utm_source=openai)).

Key features enabled by this design include: an intelligent `Learner` class that automatically selects loss functions and handles training/validation data; a two-way callback system allowing custom behavior at any point during training; a data block API for constructing data pipelines flexibly; and low-level primitives like type-dispatch and semantic tensor handling for deep customization ([fast.ai](https://www.fast.ai/posts/2020-02-13-fastai-A-Layered-API-for-Deep-Learning.html?utm_source=openai)).

The layered API reflects fastai’s mission to be both user-friendly and deeply extensible, combining the productivity of high-level frameworks with the transparency and composability valued by researchers ([mdpi.com](https://www.mdpi.com/2078-2489/11/2/108?utm_source=openai)).

sources.title: fastai A Layered API for Deep Learning – fast\.ai
Fastai: A Layered API for Deep Learning (MDPI Information journal)
Introduction to FastAI – GeeksforGeeks

fastai library.summary: fastai is an open‑source deep learning library built atop PyTorch that employs a layered API design—high‑level for ease of use, mid‑level for customization, and low‑level for full flexibility—enabling both rapid prototyping and advanced experimentation.
keyPoints: Layered architecture: high‑level APIs for vision, text, tabular, and recommendation tasks; mid‑level components like Learner, DataLoaders, and Callbacks; low‑level primitives including Transforms, semantic tensors, and type‑dispatch mechanisms ([fast.ai](https://www.fast.ai/posts/2020-02-13-fastai-A-Layered-API-for-Deep-Learning.html?utm_source=openai))

High‑level API supports rapid model training using sensible defaults (e.g., Learner class auto‑selects loss, manages optimizer and data) while still allowing full access to PyTorch under the hood ([fast.ai](https://www.fast.ai/posts/2020-02-13-fastai-A-Layered-API-for-Deep-Learning.html?utm_source=openai))

Mid‑level APIs enable powerful customization: fastai features a two‑way callback system, a flexible DataLoaders/DataBlock API, and composable layer constructs like SequentialEx and MergeLayer for modern architectures ([fast.ai](https://www.fast.ai/posts/2020-02-13-fastai-A-Layered-API-for-Deep-Learning.html?utm_source=openai))

Low‑level APIs offer functional building blocks including GPU‑optimized augmentations, transform pipelines with reversible `Transform` objects, type‑dispatch system, and convenience tools like the `L` collection and patch decorators ([fast.ai](https://www.fast.ai/posts/2020-02-13-fastai-A-Layered-API-for-Deep-Learning.html?utm_source=openai))

Distinguished Research Scientist at the.canonical: Distinguished Research Scientist at the University of San Francisco
Distinguished Research Scientist at the.summary: Jeremy Howard is an Australian data scientist, entrepreneur, and educator who held the title of Distinguished Research Scientist at the University of San Francisco. During his tenure there, he was the founding chair of the Wicklow Artificial Intelligence in Medical Research Initiative, furthering AI applications in medical research.
keyPoints: Jeremy Howard held the position of Distinguished Research Scientist at the University of San Francisco.
He served as the founding chair of the Wicklow Artificial Intelligence in Medical Research Initiative at USF.
He is also a founding researcher at fast.ai and an Honorary Professor at the University of Queensland.
He previously founded and led companies such as Enlitic and served as President and Chief Scientist of Kaggle.

Honorary Professor at the University of.canonical: Honorary Professor at the University of Queensland
Honorary Professor at the University of.summary: Jeremy Howard is an internationally renowned AI expert and co‑founder of fast.ai who has held the title of Honorary Professor at the University of Queensland’s School of Information Technology and Electrical Engineering (ITEE). Appointed in March 2022, his role at UQ includes leading practical deep learning education efforts such as offering a fast.ai short course "Practical Deep Learning for Coders", and participating in public engagement events like an "Ask Me Anything" deep learning session.
keyPoints: Jeremy Howard, co‑founder of fast.ai, was appointed Honorary Professor at UQ’s School of Information Technology and Electrical Engineering in March 2022 ([ai.uq.edu.au](https://ai.uq.edu.au/article/2022/03/renowned-ai-expert-joins-uq-help-develop-local-talent?utm_source=openai)).

As Honorary Professor, he has led delivery of the fast.ai 7‑week short course "Practical Deep Learning for Coders" at UQ starting April 2022 ([ai.uq.edu.au](https://ai.uq.edu.au/article/2022/03/renowned-ai-expert-joins-uq-help-develop-local-talent?utm_source=openai)).

Howard’s role also includes public education activities, exemplified by his appearance as “Honorary Professor Jeremy Howard” in a deep learning “Ask Me Anything” event in June 2022 ([ai.uq.edu.au](https://ai.uq.edu.au/event/session/1260?utm_source=openai)).

His Wikipedia profile confirms he teaches both online and in-person, including at UQ as an Honorary Professor in the School of Information Technology and Electrical Engineering ([en.wikipedia.org](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Jeremy_Howard_%28entrepreneur%29?utm_source=openai)).

evidence review on public masking in PNA.canonical: evidence review on public masking in PNAS
evidence review on public masking in PNA.summary: “An evidence review of face masks against COVID‑19,” authored by Jeremy Howard et al. and published in Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences (PNAS) on January 11, 2021, is a comprehensive narrative review analyzing the effectiveness of public face‑mask usage during the COVID‑19 pandemic. It synthesizes laboratory, clinical, and epidemiological evidence to inform key dimensions such as transmission dynamics, source control, wearer protection, sociological and implementation considerations. The review concludes that widespread public cloth‑mask wearing significantly reduces transmissibility per contact and should be strongly encouraged alongside hygiene, distancing, and contact tracing measures.
keyPoints: Published January 11, 2021, in PNAS (Vol 118, Issue 4) by Jeremy Howard and 18 co‑authors ([scinapse.io](https://www.scinapse.io/papers/3118643387?utm_source=openai)).

Analyzed 141 studies to develop a framework covering population-level impact, transmission characteristics, source control, wearer protection, sociological, and implementation considerations ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33431650/?utm_source=openai)).

Found that mask wearing reduces transmission per contact in both laboratory and clinical contexts, with effectiveness depending on high public compliance ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33431650/?utm_source=openai)).

Recommended public cloth‑mask wearing as effective source control, especially when medical masks are scarce, integrated with hygiene, distancing, and contact tracing strategies ([pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/33431650/?utm_source=openai)).
